title: Southern peach cobbler
subtitle: A batter-bottomed peach pudding from the American South
excerpt: The Southern American cobbler in which a thin cake batter is poured into melted butter and a layer of sugared peaches is laid on top,and the batter rises through the fruit in the oven, leaving the peaches half-suspended in golden sponge. One dish, no pastry, ready in under an hour. Best eaten warm with cream.
sourceNotes: The batter-up cobbler is documented across the American South from the late nineteenth century onward, distinct from the British cobbler shape (a scone-topped fruit pudding). USDA / NCHFP guidance on stone-fruit handling for home baking. Pre-1928 Southern community cookbooks,the Civil-War-era and Reconstruction-era charity collections held by Project Gutenberg and the Smithsonian,carry many variants of the butter-batter-fruit pattern. The proportions here are the classic 1 stick of butter / 1 cup each of flour, sugar, milk / a quart of fruit, expressed in metric.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles into a larger roasting tin; bake an extra 10 minutes and check the centre with a skewer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sugar the peaches up to 4 hours ahead and hold at room temperature; the syrup that draws out is wanted in the bake.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. A moderate temperature lets the batter rise through the fruit without scorching the surface before the centre cooks.

recipeTools.notes: 25 cm × 20 cm ceramic or glass dish, 5 cm deep.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Southern American baking technique in which melted butter is poured into a dish, a thin self-raising batter poured on top, and the sugared fruit laid over the batter without stirring. The batter rises through the fruit and butter as it bakes, producing a sponge that holds the peaches in suspension with a butter-rich golden crust. Distinct from the British cobbler, which is a fruit pudding topped with scone or biscuit dough.

content.text: The Southern peach cobbler is built upside-down. Melted butter goes in the dish first; a thin batter is poured over the butter; sugared peaches are laid over the batter without mixing. In the oven the
batter rises around and through the fruit
, the butter floods up through the gaps, and the result is a golden sponge studded with half-suspended peaches and edged in caramelised crust. Serves eight, ready in under an hour, and best warm.

Fresh ripe peaches give the best result. Out of season, frozen peaches work,defrost and drain,and a 700 g tin of peaches in juice is the next-best fallback. Drain tinned fruit well or the bake stays wet.

items.prepNote: stoned and sliced into 1 cm wedges

content.text: Toss the sliced peaches with the first quantity of sugar, the cinnamon, and the lemon juice in a bowl. Leave to sit for 15 minutes while the oven heats and the syrup begins to draw,this becomes part of the bake.
Melt the butter in the dish

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Cut the butter into a 25 cm × 20 cm baking dish and slide it into the oven for 4 to 5 minutes, until the butter has melted and is just beginning to colour at the edges. Take the dish out and set on a heatproof surface.

Whisk the flour, the second quantity of sugar, the milk, and the vanilla in a bowl until smooth,about 30 seconds with a balloon whisk. The batter is thin, the consistency of single cream. Pour the batter directly into the melted butter in the centre of the dish. Do not stir.
Spoon the sugared peaches and their syrup evenly over the batter. The fruit will sit on the surface. Do not stir or push the peaches into the batter,the rising batter does that work in the oven.

Bake for 40 to 45 minutes, until the batter has risen and split around the peaches, the top is deep gold, and the edges are crisp and caramel-coloured. A skewer pushed into the sponge between the fruit comes out with damp crumbs but no wet batter. Rest in the dish for 10 minutes before serving,the cobbler is too liquid hot from the oven and benefits from settling.
Spoon onto plates and serve warm with cold double cream or vanilla ice cream. The cobbler keeps a day at room temperature, covered; warm portions briefly under the grill before serving.
